metadata.title: Credit Card Use and Management
metadata.subtitle: Master the Tool That Can Build Wealth or Create Debt

content.title: Credit Card Use and Management
content.subtitle: Master the Tool That Can Build Wealth or Create Debt

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
James received a credit card with a $5,000 limit and quickly maxed it out on electronics, clothes, and dinners. Unable to pay the full balance, he started making only minimum payments. What mistakes did James make, and how could he have avoided this debt trap? What mistakes did James make, and how could he have avoided this debt trap?

What mistakes did James make, and how could he have avoided this debt trap?

slides.notes:
DISCUSSION PROMPTS:
THE CHALLENGE (5 min):
• Read James's story—emphasize $1,500 interest on forgotten purchases.
• Ask: "How many of you have seen credit card offers? What do they promise?"

objectives.description: Understand how credit card interest, grace periods, and minimum payments work.
Calculate the true cost of carrying a credit card balance over time.
Develop responsible credit card usage strategies that build credit without creating debt.
Evaluate credit card offers and choose cards that match your financial goals.

terms.definition: The yearly interest rate charged on unpaid credit card balances. Average is 20-25% for new cardholders.
terms.example: A 24% APR means you pay about 2% per month on any balance you carry.

terms.definition: Time (usually 21-25 days) after your statement closes to pay your balance in full before interest is charged.
terms.example: If you pay in full by the due date, you pay $0 interest—it's like a free short-term loan!

terms.definition: The smallest payment required to keep your account in good standing—usually 1-3% of balance or $25-35, whichever is greater.
terms.example: On a $2,000 balance, minimum might be $40—but most goes to interest, barely touching the principal.

terms.definition: The percentage of your available credit you're using. Below 30% is recommended; below 10% is ideal for credit scores.
terms.example: $500 balance on a $2,000 limit = 25% utilization (good). $1,800 balance = 90% (bad for score).

content.headerTitle: The Two Ways to Use Credit Cards

leftColumn.title: PAY IN FULL (Smart)
items: It's like a free short-term loan

rightColumn.title: CARRY A BALANCE (Costly)
items: Rewards cost more than they're worth

leftPanel.title: Why Minimums Keep You in Debt
paragraphs: Minimum payments are designed to keep you paying forever:
You pay MORE in interest than the original balance!
Most of each minimum payment goes to interest—only a few dollars reduce the principal.

highlightBox.text: At minimum payments, a $2,000 purchase costs you $4,200+ total. That's 2x the price!

infoCard.title: Simple Rule:
items: Better: Pay a fixed amount you can afford
Best: Pay in full every month
Never charge more than you can pay off

leftPanel.title: Keep Your Balance Low
paragraphs: High utilization signals risk to lenders—even if you pay in full each month.
Your utilization is reported when your statement closes, not when you pay.

highlightBox.text: Pro tip: Pay down your balance BEFORE the statement closes to report lower utilization.

layoutData.title: Beyond Interest Rates
paragraphs: Credit cards have many features—some helpful, some costly:
bulletPoints: Rewards/Cashback: Free money IF you pay in full. Worthless if you carry a balance (interest > rewards).
Annual Fee: Some cards charge $0, premium cards $95-$695. Only worth it if benefits exceed the fee.
Cash Advance: Borrowing cash from your card. AVOID—higher APR (25%+), no grace period, fees.
Balance Transfer: Moving debt to a lower-rate card. Watch for transfer fees (3-5%) and promotional period end.
Foreign Transaction Fee: 1-3% on purchases abroad. Get a no-fee card if you travel.
Purchase Protection: Extended warranties, fraud protection—a real benefit of credit cards over debit.

keyPoint.text: 🎯 Focus on cards with no annual fee and good basic cashback until you have a clear strategy for premium cards.

layoutData.title: How to Use Credit Cards Without Getting Burned
paragraphs: Follow these rules and credit cards become powerful tools, not traps:
bulletPoints: Never charge more than you can pay off this month: Treat it like a debit card with benefits.
Pay in full, every month: Set up autopay for the full balance—never pay interest.
Keep utilization under 30%: Better yet, under 10% for the best credit score impact.
Don't use cash advances: Expensive, immediate interest, no grace period.
Check statements monthly: Catch fraud and review your spending patterns.
Have a plan before buying: If you can't pay it off, you can't afford it (with rare exceptions).

keyPoint.text: ✨ Used right, credit cards = free 21-day loans + rewards + fraud protection + credit building. Used wrong = expensive debt trap.

slides.notes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:

1. "The minimum payment is what I should pay."
   → The minimum keeps you in good standing but maximizes interest costs. Always pay more—ideally the full balance.

2. "I need to carry a balance to build credit."
   → False. You build credit by using the card and paying it off. Carrying a balance just costs you money.

3. "Credit cards are always bad."
   → Credit cards are tools. Paid in full monthly, they provide free short-term loans, rewards, fraud protection, and credit building.

leftPanel.title: Credit Cards Build Credit History
paragraphs: Credit cards are one of the best tools for building credit because they report monthly:
Payment history (35%): Pay on time every month—even minimum counts as on-time
Credit utilization (30%): Keep balances low relative to limits
Length of credit history (15%): Keep old cards open, even unused
Credit mix (10%): Having a credit card diversifies your credit profile

infoCard.title: Starter Strategy:
items: Get a secured card or student card
Set up autopay for full balance
Keep it open as you get better cards

scenario.name: Sarah's Smart Strategy
paragraphs: Sarah uses her credit card for all regular purchases—groceries, gas, subscriptions—but never charges more than $500/month on her $3,000 limit.
She pays her full balance every month before the due date and earns 2% cashback on everything.

scenario.name: James's Mistakes
paragraphs: James maxed out his $5,000 limit on things he wanted but didn't need. Unable to pay the full balance, he started making only minimum payments.
Two years later: Still owes $4,200, paid $1,500+ in interest, credit score dropped due to 100% utilization.

content.question:
Compare Sarah and James:

1. What specific behaviors made Sarah successful and James unsuccessful?

2. If James wanted to get out of debt, what should he do right now?

3. How much would James save if he paid $150/month instead of the minimum?

Some people say 'cash is king' and credit cards are dangerous. Others say credit cards are powerful financial tools.

What's your view?

How would you use credit cards responsibly?

questions.question: What happens if you pay your credit card balance in full during the grace period?
Why is a cash advance worse than a regular credit card purchase?
Sophia's credit limit is $2,500. What's the maximum balance she should carry to stay at 30% utilization?
List two specific mistakes James made and what he should have done differently.

slides.notes:
ANSWER KEY:

1. B (You pay no interest on purchases)

2. Cash advances have higher APR (often 25%+), no grace period (interest starts immediately), and often have fees (3-5% of amount). Never a good choice.

3. $2,500 × 0.30 = $750 maximum

4. (1) He spent more than he could afford to pay off—maxed out his card. (2) He only made minimum payments. He should have only charged what he could pay in full and stopped using the card while paying down the balance.

5. Balanced responses recognize credit cards as tools. Used wisely (pay in full, low utilization), they build credit and offer rewards. Used poorly (carrying balances, overspending), they create costly debt.

takeaways.title: Pay in Full = Pay No Interest
takeaways.description: The grace period means you get a free short-term loan—but only if you pay the full balance by the due date.

takeaways.title: Minimum Payments = Debt Trap
takeaways.description: Paying minimum on $2,000 takes 9+ years and costs $2,200+ in interest. Always pay more than minimum.

takeaways.title: Keep Utilization Under 30%
takeaways.description: High utilization hurts your credit score even if you pay in full. Keep balances low relative to limits.

takeaways.title: Credit Cards Are Tools
takeaways.description: Used wisely: free loans, rewards, fraud protection, credit building. Used poorly: expensive debt trap.

main.title: Credit Card Strategy Challenge
main.description: James learned the hard way—but can you avoid his mistakes? Tomorrow you'll analyze real credit card scenarios, calculate true costs, and develop your personal credit card strategy.

steps: Analyze credit card scenarios and predict outcomes
Calculate minimum payment costs vs. accelerated payment savings
Determine optimal utilization for different credit limits
Evaluate credit card offers based on your needs
Create personal rules for responsible credit card use

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
